title: Why Is Momentus Stock Gaining Wednesday?
author: Lekha Gupta
description: Momentus (MNTS) stock rose over 6% on Wednesday following the announcement of a commercial contract with the University of Colorado Boulder's Laboratory for Atmospheric and Space Physics. The agreement covers in-orbit services for the company's Vigoride-9 Orbital Service Vehicle, with the OWLS mission scheduled to launch in 2027. The contract strengthens Momentus's hosted payload and in-orbit services business, with Vigoride-8 fully booked and Vigoride-9 still having capacity for additional customers.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: The company secured a new commercial contract with a major academic institution, demonstrating growing demand for its in-space services. The stock gained 5.91% on the announcement, and the company's commercial pipeline continues to expand with multiple Vigoride missions booked or in operation.

The company secured a new commercial contract with a major academic institution, demonstrating growing demand for its in-space services. The stock gained 5.91% on the announcement, and the company's commercial pipeline continues to expand with multiple Vigoride missions booked or in operation.
The small-cap index gained 0.87%, showing modest positive performance. It serves as a market benchmark reference rather than being directly impacted by the Momentus news.
The broad market index was down 0.05%, indicating a slightly negative day for the overall market. It serves as a benchmark reference showing Momentus outperformed the broader market.

title: S&P 500 Rebound Looks Fragile as Chip Strength Masks Thin Breadth
author: Itai Smidt
description: U.S. equities rebounded Thursday driven by semiconductor strength, with Intel, Applied Materials, and Arm Holdings surging on AI chip demand. However, gains were narrow and fragile, masked by weak breadth as small-caps declined and Oracle tumbled on disappointing cloud revenue guidance. Hot inflation data (4.2% CPI, 6.5% PPI) reinforced expectations for Fed rate hikes, keeping the Russell 2000 under pressure and limiting broader market participation.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Surged 10.3% after securing a major contract to manufacture 3 million AI chips for 2028 deployment, validating foundry ambitions and extending week's recovery of over 11%.
Rose 7.8% riding renewed enthusiasm for chip-equipment franchises as semiconductor complex recovers from recent losses.
Advanced 7.8% as part of broader semiconductor recovery, benefiting from renewed AI chip demand and equipment supplier optimism.
Trading near record $1,749 with $674 billion valuation; sole producer of EUV lithography machines essential for advanced chip production, positioned as critical supplier for massive Terafab facility.

Tumbled 11.5% despite beating headline earnings; cloud revenue of $9.91B missed $9.99B expectations, and massive capital expenditures ($55.7B, up 162%) with negative free cash flow ($23.7B) spooked markets on AI spending sustainability.

Tumbled 11.5% despite beating headline earnings; cloud revenue of $9.91B missed $9.99B expectations, and massive capital expenditures ($55.7B, up 162%) with negative free cash flow ($23.7B) spooked markets on AI spending sustainability.
Guidance miss (Q3 AI chip sales outlook of $16B vs $17.2B consensus) triggered semiconductor washout, with iShares Semiconductor ETF plunging 10% on June 6 in worst single day in years.
Down 35% year-to-date and trading near $170.92; weakness in cloud-software complex following Oracle's disappointing cloud revenue guidance.
Fell 2.5% as part of broader cloud-software complex decline following Oracle's earnings miss and AI spending concerns.
Declined 2.2% as part of cloud-software weakness following Oracle's disappointing cloud revenue and capital intensity concerns.
Slid 1.10% as small-caps with higher floating-rate debt loads and domestic cyclical exposure continue pricing in Fed holding rates higher-for-longer with December rate hike fully priced.
Modest 0.21% gain masks narrow, chip-driven advance with thin breadth; gains concentrated in mega-cap tech while energy, materials, and rate-sensitive sectors remain mixed.
Rose 0.26% but remains well below recent highs above 25,900; rebound still in early innings after 4% single-session drop and 6% decline from peak.

title: Nasdaq 100 Tumbles Over 3%, Marvell Crashes 12%: Stock Market Today
author: Piero Cingari
description: U.S. stocks declined sharply on Tuesday as investors rotated out of high-flying chip and AI stocks. The Nasdaq 100 fell 3.3%, the S&P 500 dropped 1.6%, and the Russell 2000 fell 1.3%. Oil prices collapsed 6% following a halt in Israel-Iran strikes. Markets are now pricing in a potential Fed rate hike by October rather than cuts, pressuring richly valued tech stocks. Semiconductor and optical companies led losses, while defensive and financial sectors provided some cushion.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Fell 1.3% after an early advance, experiencing downward pressure alongside broader market decline
Crashed 15.2% as part of profit-taking in AI-optical and data center infrastructure names

title: S&P 500 Selloff Looks More Like Rotation Than Market Breakdown
author: Itai Smidt
description: A stronger-than-expected May jobs report (172,000 payrolls vs. 80,000-105,000 forecast) triggered a market rotation rather than a broad selloff. The strong labor data pushed Treasury yields to 4.54%, reducing rate-cut expectations and hitting rate-sensitive tech stocks. While the Nasdaq fell 1.13% and semiconductor stocks cratered, the Russell 2000 surged 1.45% as money rotated into cyclicals and small caps. The Dow held near record highs, indicating broadening market participation rather than a systemic breakdown.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock tumbled 12% Thursday after leaving full-year AI chip targets unchanged, which markets interpreted as a miss. Continued selling Friday with additional 3% decline as the unwind fed on itself, triggering broader semiconductor selloff.

Down approximately 1% on the day, showing relative strength compared to peers but still declining. Despite weakness, held up better than other chip stocks, suggesting leadership hasn't broken but is exhaling.
Dropped more than 6% Friday, giving back a significant portion of its 25% surge earlier in the week after being floated as a potential trillion-dollar company.
Fell around 5% as the semiconductor complex unwound following Broadcom's guidance miss.
Shed nearly 3% as selling spread across all names with AI revenue exposure.

Cratered more than 11% after slashing fiscal 2026 EPS guidance and reporting 2% drop in comparable sales. Multiple analyst downgrades followed, signaling deteriorating North American performance.
Posted blowout quarter but stock was still hit, indicating positioning-driven selling rather than fundamental weakness. Reflects bifurcated consumer spending patterns.
Led the Dow with 5%-plus surge as healthcare benefited from rotation out of tech into non-tech sectors.

title: These AI Megacap ETFs Are Crushing The Small-Cap Comeback Story
author: Chandrima Sanyal
description: Citigroup argues that artificial intelligence-driven earnings growth continues to favor large-cap technology stocks over small-caps. With S&P 500 companies posting 27% year-over-year earnings growth in Q1 and over half driven by AI spending, mega-cap firms maintain premium valuations. Meanwhile, smaller companies face headwinds from high borrowing costs and weak pricing power, suggesting the anticipated small-cap rebound may not fully materialize.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Heavily exposed to AI-linked mega-cap leaders benefiting from strong AI-driven earnings growth and continued investor focus on large-cap technology stocks.
Provides exposure to Nasdaq-listed technology and growth companies that are driving earnings momentum through AI infrastructure spending.
Benefits from strong S&P 500 earnings growth (27% YoY) largely driven by technology and AI-linked companies.
Concentrated exposure to mega-cap growth leaders with strong pricing power and earnings momentum in the AI-driven growth regime.
Small-cap focused fund facing headwinds from high borrowing costs, weak pricing power, margin pressure, and sensitivity to interest rate expectations.
